displayTitle: Indication, Interpretation, Evaluation — In That Order

beats.narration: Something on a weld catches your eye. What you call it next carries legal weight. Visual testing has a strict ladder of words.

payload.title: CALL IT CORRECTLY
payload.sub: Indication, interpretation, evaluation — in that order.

beats.narration: First, an indication: anything that draws your attention. Then interpretation: what caused it? Then evaluation: measured against the acceptance criteria.
Interpretation gives three answers. False: surface or setup, like a grease smear. Nonrelevant: a real but harmless feature. Relevant: an actual discontinuity.

chips.s: A grease smear, not the part

beats.narration: Evaluation measures the relevant indication and compares it against the acceptance criteria. Fail that comparison, and it earns the word defect: rejectable, by definition.
Defect is a verdict, not a synonym. Loose use can condemn good hardware. So here's the rule: never say defect until the criteria have spoken.

payload.sub: Defect is a verdict — until the criteria have spoken, it's an indication.
